optionLabels.clearly_domestic: Program A — save 10 lives in your country
optionLabels.hard_to_say: Hard to say — I'd want to know more before choosing
optionLabels.clearly_abroad: Program B — save 100 lives in a developing nation

questions.promptFull: You picked the abroad option just now — a wide moral circle. Even so, some groups may still pull at you. Sort each by whether you feel extra moral concern for them, beyond what you feel for people in general.

optionLabels.ingroup_religious: People who share your religious tradition
optionLabels.ingroup_ethnic_racial: People of your racial or ethnic background
optionLabels.ingroup_ideological: People who share your core political values

optionLabels.neighbor: Neighbor — lives next door, small talk at the mailbox
optionLabels.coworker: Coworker — shares a team or office with you every day
optionLabels.close_friend: Close friend — someone you confide in regularly
optionLabels.sibling_inlaw: Sibling-in-law — part of your family through marriage
optionLabels.dating_partner: Your romantic partner — someone you are dating exclusively
optionLabels.spouse: Your spouse — lifelong partner and co-parent

questions.promptFull: Rank these ways of deciding what is politically true from most like you to least like you.

optionLabels.eps_empiricist: Evidence-first - studies, data, measurement, and observable results
optionLabels.eps_institutionalist: Institutional confidence - expert consensus, courts, agencies, journals, and professional standards
optionLabels.eps_traditionalist: Inherited wisdom - long-standing traditions, religious teaching, and practices that have endured
optionLabels.eps_intuitionist: Moral intuition - conscience, gut sense, and felt right-or-wrong
optionLabels.eps_autonomous: Independent reasoning - work it out myself; no authority gets automatic deference
optionLabels.eps_nihilist: Radical skepticism - most sources are compromised, so claims need deep suspicion

questions.promptFull: Pick the political leadership style you find most appealing and the one you find least appealing.

optionLabels.aes_statesman: Statesman - formal and dignified, measured tone, ceremonial register, presidential bearing
optionLabels.aes_technocrat: Technocrat - precise and analytical, expert-coded delivery, comfortable with technical detail and jargon
optionLabels.aes_pastoral: Pastoral - warm hometown register, regional cadence, family-and-community language, evocative and emotional
optionLabels.aes_authentic: Authentic - direct and casual, unscripted, refuses polish and political theater, talks like a regular person
optionLabels.aes_fighter: Fighter - combative tone, willing to attack opponents directly, confrontational delivery, doesn't soften
optionLabels.aes_visionary: Visionary - lyrical and aspirational, evocative-imagistic rhetoric, paints pictures with words

optionLabels.born_here: Being born in the country — having citizenship by birth
optionLabels.speak_lang: Being able to speak the national language
optionLabels.shared_values: Believing in the core civic values (liberty, equality, rule of law)
optionLabels.civic_part: Participating in civic life — voting, jury duty, community involvement
optionLabels.cultural: Adopting cultural customs, holidays, and traditions
optionLabels.ancestry: Having ancestral roots in the country going back generations
optionLabels.religion: Sharing the country's religious heritage and traditions
optionLabels.economic: Contributing economically — holding a job, paying taxes, not being a burden

questions.promptFull: Sort each political topic by how much it enters into your politics. Topics that feel central to your political identity go on the left. Topics you don't consider much go on the right.

optionLabels.mat: Economic life — how wealth is produced, who collects it, and how policy reshapes the distribution.
optionLabels.cd: Direction of the culture — the pace and shape of change on family, gender, sexuality, marriage, and religion.
optionLabels.cu: Common culture and difference — how a single political community accommodates many ways of life and belief, or whether it asks for one shared standard.
optionLabels.mor: Reach of moral concern — how wide a circle of obligation reaches: family, country, all of humanity, or somewhere in between.
optionLabels.pro: Procedure and expedience — the weight of fair rules and due process against the urgency of getting the right result.
optionLabels.com: Dealmaking and conviction — the terms on which political actors give ground, hold the line, or refuse to deal.
optionLabels.zs: Cooperation and conflict — whether gains in economic, political, and cultural life come from working together or from one side winning at another's expense.
optionLabels.ont_h: Human nature — the weight of disposition versus environment and education in shaping people's character.
optionLabels.ont_s: Institutions — the role of formal organizations (laws, courts, agencies, treaties) in producing stability, progress, and accountability.
optionLabels.eps: Evidence and authority — the standards by which political claims earn credibility: data, tradition, expert opinion, lived experience, gut instinct.
optionLabels.aes: Political style — the kind of leadership and rhetorical register that resonates: fighter, statesman, visionary, technocrat, pragmatist.

questions.promptFull: Which best describes how you think of your political affiliation?
When citizens, legal residents, minorities, or other groups living under the country's laws are denied equal moral or legal standing, how central is fixing that to your politics?

Setting aside how well institutions function right now, how essential do you think strong institutions — laws, courts, civic organizations, international bodies — are to a society's long-term flourishing?
Looking at major political progress in the United States over the last 100 years, where has it MOSTLY come from?
If you were prioritizing one approach to strengthen American democracy today, which would do MORE?
How much extra moral concern do you feel for each of these groups, beyond what you feel for people in general? Sort each into the column that fits best.

optionLabels.ingroup_ideological: People who share your core political values

questions.promptFull: How much moral concern do you feel for any human being, simply because they are human?
When a stranger is suffering, how much does that matter to you even if they share none of your identities, beliefs, nationality, or politics?
Compared with people in general, how much extra moral concern do you feel for people in your country?
Compared with people in general, how much extra moral concern do you feel for people who share your religious tradition or sacred worldview?
Compared with people in general, how much extra moral concern do you feel for people who share your racial or ethnic background?
Compared with people in general, how much extra moral concern do you feel for people in your economic class or material situation?
Compared with people in general, how much extra moral concern do you feel for people who share your gender?
Compared with people in general, how much extra moral concern do you feel for people who share your core ideology, values, or political side?
